story.timeline_note: During Kṛṣṇa's childhood in Vraja, after he goes to the forest with the cows.
story.comic_potential: The gopīs' exaggerated descriptions of nature's reactions to Kṛṣṇa's flute, such as cows standing still with chewed grass in their mouths, rivers stopping their flow, and trees bowing down, have a humorous, whimsical quality.

teachings.teaching: The divine sound of Kṛṣṇa's flute captivates all beings, drawing them to him in devotion.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates how the inner sound (nāda) can focus the mind and awaken spiritual longing, leading to union with the divine.

teachings.teaching: True devotion involves complete absorption in the divine, forgetting worldly concerns.
teachings.lens_note: The gopīs' state of being 'vimukta-gṛhāśāḥ' (free from household desires) exemplifies the Kriya-Yoga ideal of detachment through constant remembrance of God.

teachings.teaching: Nature itself responds to the presence of the divine, reflecting the interconnectedness of all creation.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga principle that the entire universe is a manifestation of consciousness, and spiritual practice harmonizes one with that cosmic rhythm.

chapter_summary: Krishna kills the demon Ariṣṭa in the form of a bull, then Nārada informs Kaṃsa that Krishna and Balarāma are the sons of Vasudeva and Devakī, destined to kill him. Kaṃsa imprisons Vasudeva and Devakī, sends Keśī to kill the brothers, and plots to have them killed by the elephant Kuvalayāpīḍa and wrestlers during a bow sacrifice. He sends Akrūra to bring them to Mathurā.

story.title: The Killing of Ariṣṭa and Kaṃsa's Plot
story.arc: Krishna's childhood pastimes in Vraja and the prelude to his journey to Mathurā
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the killing of Pralamba and before the bow sacrifice in Mathurā.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic bull-demon fight, Nārada's revelation, Kaṃsa's rage, and the suspense of Akrūra's mission.

teachings.teaching: Krishna protects his devotees and destroys demons who terrorize them.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the inner purification where the divine will (Krishna) eliminates negative tendencies (demons) that disturb the mind (gopas and gopīs).

teachings.teaching: The divine plan unfolds through apparent adversities; Kaṃsa's fear leads to his own destruction.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The ego (Kaṃsa) tries to suppress the higher self (Krishna) but only accelerates its own dissolution. Surrender to the divine will is the path to liberation.

teachings.teaching: Akrūra's response: one should act without attachment to results, recognizing that destiny is the ultimate doer.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This echoes the principle of karma-yoga — performing one's duty with equanimity, surrendering the fruits to the divine. Akrūra exemplifies the ideal practitioner.

chapter_summary: Krishna kills the demon Keshī in horse form, then kills the demon Vyoma (son of Maya) who had kidnapped cowherd boys. The sage Nārada visits Krishna, praises him, and prophesies his future exploits including the killing of Kaṃsa and other demons.

story.title: Krishna Slays the Demons Keshī and Vyoma
story.arc: Krishna's childhood pastimes in Vraja
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Keshī, Nārada visits and prophesies future events including the killing of Kaṃsa 'the day after tomorrow' (paraśvo 'hani). Then later, Vyoma's abduction occurs.
story.comic_potential: The fight with Keshī is dramatic: a giant horse demon terrorizing Gokula, Krishna dodging kicks, then thrusting his arm into its mouth and expanding it. The Vyoma episode has a playful hide-and-seek game turning into a kidnapping, with Krishna rescuing the boys from a cave.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the supreme self (ātman) of all beings, the witness in the heart, and the controller of māyā.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this affirms that the inner self is identical to Krishna, and meditation on the heart's witness leads to liberation.

teachings.teaching: Krishna descends to protect the righteous and destroy demons.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the divine descends whenever there is a need to re-establish dharma, and the practitioner should invoke Krishna's protection in their own spiritual battle.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's actions are effortless (līlā) and he remains undisturbed even while killing demons.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the ideal of acting without attachment, a key principle in Kriya-Yoga: perform actions as an offering, remaining inwardly calm.

teachings.teaching: The sage Nārada's devotion and prophecy show the importance of associating with devotees and hearing about Krishna's glories.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma encourages regular satsaṅga and study of scriptures to strengthen one's connection to the divine.

chapter_summary: Akrūra travels from Mathurā to Gokula, reflecting on his good fortune to see Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma. He arrives, sees their footprints, and meets them. They welcome him, offer hospitality, and Nanda inquires about Kamsa's tyranny.

story.title: Akrūra's Journey to Gokula
story.arc: Akrūra, sent by Kamsa, travels to Gokula to see Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma. He is overwhelmed with devotion, meets them, and is warmly received.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Kamsa's plot to kill Kṛṣṇa, before the wrestling match in Mathurā.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's internal monologue of ecstatic devotion contrasts with his mission as Kamsa's spy, creating dramatic irony.

teachings.teaching: Seeing the Lord is the highest blessing, even for a lowly person.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the grace of the guru (sādhu-saṅga) which purifies the mind and leads to self-realization.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's feet are worshipped by all gods and sages; even the dust of His feet is supremely purifying.
teachings.lens_note: Symbolizes the importance of surrendering to the divine will (īśvara-praṇidhāna) in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial, yet He reciprocates with His devotees according to their devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle that the divine responds to sincere practice (sādhana).

teachings.teaching: The purpose of human life is to give up pride, fear, and sorrow by seeing, hearing, and serving the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Aligns with Kriya-Yoga's aim of transcending the ego through meditation and devotion.

chapter_summary: Akrura arrives in Vrindavan to take Krishna and Balarama to Mathura for the festival. The gopis lament Krishna's departure, expressing their love and despair. Krishna and Balarama leave with Akrura, and on the way, Akrura sees a vision of the serpent Ananta and the four-armed Vishnu in the Yamuna river.

story.title: Akrura's Arrival and the Gopis' Lament
story.arc: Krishna's departure from Vrindavan to Mathura
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Putana and other demons, before the slaying of Kamsa.
story.comic_potential: High: The gopis' dramatic lament, Akrura's vision, and the departure scene are visually and emotionally rich.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' intense love for Krishna illustrates the pinnacle of devotion (bhakti), where the devotee feels complete separation (viraha) and cannot bear to be without the divine.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this viraha is the highest stage of bhakti, where the practitioner's entire being is absorbed in the divine, leading to spontaneous kriya and union.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's assurance to the gopis that he will return ('āyāsya iti') shows that the divine never abandons the devotee; separation is temporary and for a higher purpose.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the divine's apparent absence is a test to deepen the practitioner's longing and practice, ultimately leading to a more profound realization.

teachings.teaching: Akrura's vision of Vishnu and Ananta in the Yamuna reveals that the divine is present everywhere, and the external journey is also an internal one.
teachings.lens_note: This vision corresponds to the Kriya-Yoga experience of inner awakening, where the practitioner sees the cosmic form within through meditation.

chapter_summary: Akrura, upon seeing Krishna in his divine form, is overwhelmed with devotion and offers a prayer of praise, describing the Lord's attributes and attendants.

story.title: Akrura's Vision and Prayer
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's return to Dvaraka, Akrura sees his divine form.
story.comic_potential: Akrura's overwhelmed reaction and the vivid description of Krishna's divine attendants and energies.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is served by various divine energies and devotees, indicating his supreme position.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the integration of all energies (kriyas) in service to the divine self.

teachings.teaching: Devotion leads to direct vision of the Lord's form.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that through sustained kriya-yoga practice, the practitioner can perceive the divine presence directly.

teachings.teaching: Prayer with heartfelt emotion and humility is the proper response to divine vision.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's teaching that kriya-yoga culminates in bhakti, where the practitioner offers all actions to the divine.

chapter_summary: Akrūra offers a profound prayer to Kṛṣṇa, acknowledging Him as the supreme cause of all causes, the source of the universe, and the ultimate object of all forms of worship. He describes the cosmic form of the Lord, enumerates His avatāras, and confesses his own ignorance and bondage to māyā, seeking refuge at Kṛṣṇa's lotus feet.

story.title: Akrūra's Prayer to Kṛṣṇa
story.arc: Akrūra, having arrived in Vṛndāvana, offers a heartfelt prayer to Kṛṣṇa, acknowledging His supremacy and seeking refuge.
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs during Akrūra's visit to Vṛndāvana, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's ecstatic prayer and the vivid descriptions of Kṛṣṇa's avatāras could be visually stunning.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate cause of all causes, and all beings, including demigods, are parts of His body.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that all actions and energies originate from the one supreme consciousness, and the practitioner should see the divine in all.

teachings.teaching: The material elements and senses are not the true self; the self is beyond the guṇas.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that through meditation, one can transcend the guṇas and realize the ātman as distinct from prakṛti.

teachings.teaching: All forms of worship, whether Vedic, tantric, or devotional, ultimately reach the same Supreme Lord.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's inclusive approach, where various paths are seen as valid means to connect with the divine.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's avatāras are manifestations of His playful pastimes for the benefit of the world.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views avatāras as archetypal energies that can be invoked through meditation and mantra.

teachings.teaching: The conditioned soul is bewildered by māyā, identifying with the body and possessions, and thus suffers in saṃsāra.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga aims to break this identification through self-inquiry and meditation, leading to liberation.

teachings.teaching: Surrender to the Lord is the ultimate means to overcome the mind and senses.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, surrender (prapatti) is a key practice, where the practitioner offers all actions to the divine.

chapter_summary: Akrūra witnesses Krishna's divine form in the Yamuna, then returns to Mathura with Krishna and Balarāma. They enter the city, are welcomed by the citizens, and encounter a washerman who refuses to give them clothes; Krishna kills him. A weaver then dresses them beautifully, and they visit a florist named Sudāmā, who offers them garlands and receives blessings.

story.title: Akrūra's Vision and Entry into Mathurā
story.arc: Krishna's journey to Mathurā and encounters with citizens
story.timeline_note: After the vision in the Yamunā, same day entry into Mathurā.
story.comic_potential: The washerman's arrogant refusal and swift beheading; the weaver's joyful transformation; the women's frantic rush to see Krishna.

teachings.teaching: The Lord reveals his divine form to his devotee as a sign of grace.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the inner vision of the Self (ātma-darśana) attained through sustained practice.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial and equal to all beings, yet shows special favor to devotees.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that divine grace is available to all who surrender, but the devotee's love draws the Lord's response.

teachings.teaching: Service to the Lord and his devotees is the highest path.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga teaches that selfless service (seva) purifies the mind and leads to realization.

chapter_summary: Bhagavata Purana Chapter 41 (part 2) describes Krishna granting a boon to a devotee, bestowing prosperity, strength, longevity, fame, and beauty, and then departing with his elder brother.

story.title: Krishna Grants a Boon
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After the boon, Krishna and Balarama depart.
story.comic_potential: The boon-giving scene can be depicted with Krishna's divine grace and the devotee's joy.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Krishna leads to material and spiritual blessings.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this boon represents the fruits of disciplined practice and surrender.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama enter Mathura, encounter the hunchbacked woman Trivakra who offers them sandalwood paste, and Krishna straightens her back. They then break the great bow in the arena, kill the guards, and later learn of Kamsa's plans. Kamsa, terrified by omens, organizes a wrestling festival. The chapter ends with the gopis longing for Krishna.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama in Mathura
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Mathura leading to the killing of Kamsa
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Putana and other demons in Vrindavan, Krishna and Balarama visit Mathura for the first time.
story.comic_potential: The scene where Krishna straightens Trivakra's back and her subsequent infatuation has comedic elements.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's touch can transform and elevate a humble devotee, as seen with Trivakra.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the awakening of kundalini through divine grace, straightening the spiritual path.

teachings.teaching: The breaking of the bow represents the destruction of ego and material pride.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the breaking of the subtle body's knots (granthis) through yogic practice.

teachings.teaching: Kamsa's omens and fear illustrate the torment of a sinful mind.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the state of a person who has not purified their consciousness; in Kriya-Yoga, it is the result of blocked energy centers.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama, having heard the wrestling arena's drums, go to the arena. Krishna kills the elephant Kuvalayapida, then enters the arena with Balarama. The people praise them, recounting Krishna's past deeds. Canura challenges them to wrestle, and Krishna agrees, insisting on fair play.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama Enter the Arena
story.arc: Kamsa's downfall
story.timeline_note: After the killing of various demons in Vrindavan, Krishna and Balarama go to Mathura for the wrestling festival.
story.comic_potential: High: elephant fight, crowd reactions, dramatic entry.

teachings.teaching: One should act in accordance with dharma, even in competition.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes righteous action (dharma) as the foundation of spiritual practice. Krishna insists on fair wrestling, showing that even in battle, dharma must be upheld.

teachings.teaching: The divine can be recognized through extraordinary deeds.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's lens sees the recognition of Krishna's divinity by the people as a result of his pastimes (lila), which reveal his transcendental nature. This inspires devotion and surrender.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama defeat and kill the wrestlers Canura, Mustika, Kuta, Sala, and Tosala in the arena of Kamsa. Kamsa orders the arrest of Vasudeva, Nanda, and Ugrasena, but Krishna leaps onto the royal dais, grabs Kamsa by the hair, and throws him down, killing him. Kamsa's brothers attack and are slain by Balarama. The women of Kamsa's family lament. Krishna and Balarama then release their parents and bow to them.

story.title: The Slaying of Kamsa
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Mathura
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs after Krishna and Balarama have arrived in Mathura and are challenged by Kamsa's wrestlers.
story.comic_potential: High: The wrestling match, the dramatic leap onto the dais, and the killing of Kamsa are visually striking and action-packed.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects the righteous and destroys the wicked; even a king who commits adharma will be punished.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of karma: actions driven by ego and adharma lead to self-destruction, while surrender to the divine (kriya) brings liberation.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' devotion is the highest; they see Krishna's beauty and hear his flute, which purifies them.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the gopis represent the soul's intense longing for union with the divine; their spontaneous love is the model for kriya-yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: One should not associate with a sabha (assembly) where adharma prevails; a wise person avoids such places.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's teaching that the seeker must avoid environments that disturb inner stillness and kriya practice.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama return to Mathura, reunite with their parents Vasudeva and Devaki, and console them. They install Ugrasena as king, welcome back the Yadava clans, and send Nanda and the gopas back to Vrindavan. They undergo sacred thread ceremony, study under Sandipani, and retrieve the guru's son from Yamaloka, demonstrating their divine nature.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama's Return to Mathura and Education
story.arc: Krishna's early life and education
story.timeline_note: After Kaṃsa's death, Krishna and Balarama return to Mathura, reunite with parents, install Ugrasena, send Nanda back, undergo upanayana, study under Sandipani, and retrieve guru's son.
story.comic_potential: The retrieval of the guru's son from Yamaloka, with Krishna blowing the conch and Yama's respectful response, has dramatic and humorous elements.

teachings.teaching: One must serve parents and elders; failing to do so leads to suffering in the afterlife.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of selfless service (seva) as a foundational practice for purifying the mind and preparing for higher yoga.

teachings.teaching: The guru's fee (guru-dakṣiṇā) must be fulfilled; here Krishna and Balarama retrieve the guru's son from death.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga highlights the guru's role as a conduit for divine grace; fulfilling the guru's request with devotion accelerates spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: Even though Krishna and Balarama are omniscient, they undergo human-like education to set an example.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the concept of līlā (divine play) in Kriya-Yoga: the divine descends to teach humans the path of dharma and yoga through apparent human limitations.

chapter_summary: Uddhava is sent by Krishna to Vrindavan to console Nanda, Yashoda, and the gopis who are suffering from separation. Uddhava delivers Krishna's message, describing his divine nature and assuring them of his return. The chapter highlights the intense love and devotion of the Vrajavasis.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to Vrindavan
story.arc: Krishna's separation and the devotion of the Vrajavasis
story.timeline_note: After Krishna killed Kamsa and settled in Mathura, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: The emotional reunion and the vivid description of Vrindavan's pastoral life.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the Supreme Soul and the cause of all causes; he is beyond material qualities yet engages in pastimes for the protection of the righteous.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes that the ultimate reality (Krishna) is both transcendent and immanent, and that devotion (bhakti) is the highest yoga, leading to union with the divine through constant remembrance.

teachings.teaching: One who fixes the mind on Krishna at the time of death, even for a moment, attains liberation.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga practice of remembering the divine at the final moment, which is a key technique for achieving moksha.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial; he has no favorites or enemies, and he is the inner self of all beings.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that the practitioner must cultivate equal vision, seeing the Lord in all, which is a prerequisite for advanced meditation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's actions are for the sake of play and protection of the devotees, not for any personal gain.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga understanding that divine incarnations are spontaneous expressions of grace, not bound by karma.

chapter_summary: Uddhava visits the gopis of Vrindavan as a messenger from Krishna. The gopis express their intense longing and pain of separation, recounting Krishna's pastimes and questioning his faithfulness. Uddhava delivers Krishna's message, which includes philosophical teachings on the nature of the self and the purpose of separation to deepen devotion. The gopis, though comforted, remain immersed in thoughts of Krishna.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to the Gopis
story.arc: Krishna's separation and message to the gopis
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura, Uddhava is sent to Vrindavan to console the gopis.
story.comic_potential: The gopis' dramatic lamentations and Uddhava's attempts to soothe them with philosophical discourse.

teachings.teaching: The self (atman) is pure consciousness, distinct from the body and mind, and is unaffected by the three states of waking, dream, and deep sleep.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the witness consciousness (sakshi) through meditative absorption, where one disidentifies from the mind-body complex.

teachings.teaching: Separation from the beloved (Krishna) is a means to intensify devotion and focus the mind entirely on him.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the pain of separation (viraha) can be transformed into a powerful catalyst for inner union, as the mind becomes single-pointed on the divine.

teachings.teaching: True renunciation is not physical abandonment but inner detachment; the gopis exemplify this by giving up everything mentally for Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, renunciation (tyaga) is primarily internal—letting go of attachments in the mind while performing one's duties, as the gopis did while still in Vraja.

teachings.teaching: The highest happiness is in freedom from hope (nairashya), as illustrated by the prostitute Pingala.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that desirelessness (nishkama) is the foundation of inner peace; the gopis acknowledge this truth yet find it hard to give up hope for Krishna.

chapter_summary: Uddhava stays in Vrindavan for several months, consoling the gopis by singing Krishna's pastimes. He praises the gopis' unparalleled devotion, which surpasses that of yogis and even goddess Lakshmi. The gopis and Nanda's family request blessings for unwavering devotion. Uddhava returns to Mathura and reports to Krishna, Balarama, and Vasudeva.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to Vrindavan
story.arc: Krishna's separation and the gopis' devotion
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Uddhava's praise of the gopis and their emotional farewell.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' devotion to Krishna is supreme, surpassing even that of yogis and goddess Lakshmi.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the pinnacle of bhakti-yoga where the practitioner's entire being is absorbed in the divine, transcending all worldly attachments and even the desire for liberation.

teachings.teaching: The gopis abandoned family and social norms to attain Krishna's lotus feet, which is sought even by the Vedas.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that true devotion requires renouncing not just external attachments but also internal ego, aligning with the Kriya-Yoga path of inner purification.

teachings.teaching: The dust of the gopis' feet purifies the three worlds; Uddhava offers obeisances to them.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the power of pure devotees; in Kriya-Yoga, association with advanced practitioners accelerates spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The gopis and Nanda's family pray for constant remembrance of Krishna in mind, speech, and body.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that all actions should be offered to the divine, integrating devotion into every aspect of life.

chapter_summary: Krishna visits the courtesan's house to fulfill her desire, then goes to Akrura's house. Akrura praises Krishna and is sent to Hastinapura to check on the Pandavas.

story.title: Krishna's Visit to Akrura and the Mission to Hastinapura
story.arc: Krishna fulfills the courtesan's desire, then visits Akrura, who praises him. Krishna sends Akrura to Hastinapura to check on the Pandavas.
story.timeline_note: After Kamsa's death, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's playful visit to the courtesan and Akrura's heartfelt praise offer contrasting tones.

teachings.teaching: The Lord fulfills the desires of his devotees, even material ones, as a means to elevate them.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates how the Guru uses desire as a stepping stone to higher consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is beyond the three gunas and not bound by karma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes transcending the gunas through inner practice.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to protect dharma and destroy adharma.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view of divine intervention to restore balance.

teachings.teaching: Saints purify by their mere sight, unlike holy waters or idols.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga values the living Guru's presence over external rituals.

chapter_summary: Akrūra visits Hastinapura to assess the situation of the Pāṇḍavas. He meets with Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Bhīṣma, Vidura, Kuntī, and others. Kuntī laments her plight and prays to Kṛṣṇa. Akrūra advises Dhṛtarāṣṭra to treat the Pāṇḍavas equally, but Dhṛtarāṣṭra admits his attachment to his sons. Akrūra returns to Dvārakā and reports to Rāma and Kṛṣṇa.

story.title: Akrūra's Mission to Hastinapura
story.arc: Pre-Kurukshetra diplomacy and Kuntī's lament
story.timeline_note: After the Pāṇḍavas' exile, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's diplomatic visit, Kuntī's emotional outburst, Dhṛtarāṣṭra's inner conflict.

teachings.teaching: One is born alone, dies alone, and experiences the fruits of one's actions alone.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the individual nature of karma and the need for personal spiritual practice (sadhana) without attachment to others.

teachings.teaching: Wealth acquired unjustly is taken by others; one who accumulates it ignorantly is abandoned by wealth, life, and family, and falls into darkness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a warning against adharmic actions that bind the soul; true wealth is spiritual merit earned through disciplined yoga.

teachings.teaching: See this world as a dream, illusion, and mental construct; control the mind and become equal and peaceful.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's Kriya-Yoga goal of stilling the mind through pranayama and meditation to realize the transient nature of the world.

teachings.teaching: No one can change the will of the Lord, who descends to relieve the earth's burden.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as acknowledging divine intervention; the yogi surrenders to the divine will, understanding that liberation comes through grace.

chapter_summary: Jarāsandha, king of Magadha, attacks Mathurā with a vast army to avenge his son-in-law Kaṃsa's death. Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, understanding their divine purpose to relieve the earth's burden, engage in battle. Kṛṣṇa decimates the enemy forces, while Balarāma captures Jarāsandha but releases him at Kṛṣṇa's behest. Jarāsandha returns seventeen times, each time defeated. On the eighteenth, the Yavana king Kālayavana, sent by Nārada, besieges Mathurā. Kṛṣṇa builds a fortress city in the sea to protect the Yadus.

story.title: Jarāsandha's Invasion and the Building of Dvārakā
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's early life and establishment of Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: After Kaṃsa's death, Jarāsandha attacks Mathurā seventeen times; on the eighteenth, Kālayavana also attacks.
story.comic_potential: High: epic battles, divine weapons, strategic retreat, and miraculous city-building.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to relieve the earth's burden and protect the righteous.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of dharma-rakṣaṇa: the divine descends to restore cosmic balance when adharma prevails.

teachings.teaching: A true hero does not boast but demonstrates prowess through action.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes kriya (action) over vāda (words); this aligns with the yogic ideal of silent, effective action.

teachings.teaching: Strategic withdrawal and protection of dependents is wise, not cowardly.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates viveka (discernment) in choosing battles and preserving resources for higher purposes.

chapter_summary: Krishna establishes his capital Dvaraka, receiving divine gifts from the gods, and then goes out unarmed to meet the approaching army.

story.title: Establishment of Dvaraka and Divine Gifts
story.arc: Krishna's earthly reign
story.timeline_note: After Krishna settled in Dvaraka, the gods presented him with gifts.
story.comic_potential: Krishna going out unarmed with a garland, despite the impending battle, has a calm and humorous contrast.

teachings.teaching: The divine gifts (Sudharma, Parijata, horses, treasures) symbolize that a realized soul receives all necessities without effort.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that when one is established in the Self, the universe provides support spontaneously, reflecting the principle of 'yoga-kshema'.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's unarmed approach shows that true strength lies in inner peace, not weapons.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes non-attachment and inner stillness; Krishna's action exemplifies acting without ego, trusting in divine will.

chapter_summary: Mucukunda, a great king from the Ikshvaku dynasty, had been guarding the gods in a cave for a long time. After being granted a boon, he chose to sleep. The Yavana king pursued Krishna into the cave, where Mucukunda's angry glance burned him to ashes. Krishna then revealed himself to Mucukunda, who recognized him as Narayana and offered prayers, lamenting his past attachment to worldly pleasures.

story.title: Mucukunda's Boon and the Burning of Yavana
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes with Mucukunda
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Kamsa and other demons, during Krishna's earthly pastimes.
story.comic_potential: The chase scene where Krishna leads Yavana into the cave, and the dramatic moment when Mucukunda's glance incinerates Yavana, followed by the revelation of Krishna's divine form.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's names, births, and activities are infinite and cannot be fully enumerated.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the ananta (infinite) nature of the divine, which is realized through continuous practice and surrender.

teachings.teaching: One should not be attached to worldly pleasures and family, as they lead to suffering; instead, one should seek the Lord's lotus feet.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches detachment from sense objects and focusing on inner meditation to attain liberation.

teachings.teaching: Time, as a manifestation of the Lord, eventually destroys all attachments and pride.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga understanding of kala (time) as a force that purifies the soul when one is aware of its presence.

chapter_summary: King Mucukunda, after being awakened by Krishna, realizes the futility of worldly pleasures and power. He praises Krishna, expresses his desire for liberation, and Krishna grants him unwavering devotion and eventual liberation in a future birth as a brahmana.

story.title: Mucukunda's Liberation
story.arc: Mucukunda, after a long sleep, meets Krishna and renounces his kingdom, seeking liberation. Krishna grants him unwavering devotion and promises future liberation.
story.timeline_note: After the battle with Kālayavana, Krishna meets Mucukunda.
story.comic_potential: Mucukunda's realization of the futility of worldly power and his heartfelt prayer to Krishna.

teachings.teaching: Worldly pleasures and power are temporary and lead to suffering; true happiness is in devotion to God.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this echoes the need to transcend sensory attachments and realize the Self through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: Association with saints (sat-saṅga) leads to devotion and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the importance of satsaṅga in purifying the mind and awakening spiritual insight.

teachings.teaching: A true devotee does not desire anything other than service to the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's goal of desireless action and surrender to the divine will.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to God is unshakable even when tempted by material boons.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework teaches that steady devotion (ekānta-bhakti) is the highest form of yoga.

teachings.teaching: Even a king can attain liberation by dedicating his actions to God.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga emphasizes performing one's duties without attachment, as a form of worship.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the events following Krishna's departure from the cave where he met Mucukunda. Krishna returns to Dvaraka, defeats Jarāsandha's army, and then retreats with Balarāma to a mountain. Jarāsandha sets the mountain on fire, but they escape and return to Dvaraka. Meanwhile, King Raivata offers his daughter Revatī to Balarāma. Krishna then marries Rukmiṇī, the princess of Vidarbha, after defeating her brother Rukmī and other kings who opposed the match. The chapter includes Rukmiṇī's message to Krishna expressing her love and her plan for him to abduct her during a temple festival.

story.title: Krishna's Marriage to Rukmiṇī
story.arc: Krishna's Pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: After Mucukunda's departure, Krishna returns to Dvaraka, then faces Jarāsandha, and later marries Rukmiṇī.
story.comic_potential: The escape from the burning mountain and the daring abduction of Rukmiṇī from the temple festival are visually dramatic.

teachings.teaching: Contentment is the highest wealth; even a poor contented man sleeps happily, while a discontented king suffers.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, contentment (santoṣa) is a key niyama; it stabilizes the mind for deeper meditation.

teachings.teaching: A brāhmaṇa who is content with whatever comes is the fulfiller of all desires, as he does not deviate from dharma.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on svadharma and inner satisfaction as the foundation for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: One should bow to sages who are content with their lot, friendly to all beings, free from ego, and peaceful.
teachings.lens_note: These qualities are cultivated through Kriya-Yoga practice, leading to a state of egolessness and universal friendliness.

teachings.teaching: A king who protects his subjects so they live happily is dear to the sage.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of righteous action (dharma) in one's role, contributing to societal harmony.

teachings.teaching: Rukmiṇī expresses that hearing Krishna's qualities removes the body's distress, and seeing his form is the ultimate gain for the eyes.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, śravaṇa (hearing) and darśana (seeing) of the divine are powerful means to purify the mind and attain samādhi.

teachings.teaching: Rukmiṇī declares that she has chosen Krishna as her husband and offers herself; she prays that Śiśupāla not touch her.
teachings.lens_note: This symbolizes the soul's surrender to the divine, a key aspect of bhakti in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Rukmiṇī states that if Krishna is pleased with her worship, he should take her hand; otherwise, she will fast to death.
teachings.lens_note: This extreme determination reflects the Kriya-Yoga practitioner's unwavering resolve to attain union with the divine.

chapter_summary: Krishna, having received Rukmini's message, sets out to abduct her before her forced marriage to Shishupala. He arrives in Kundina with Balarama and the Yadava army. Rukmini, anxious, prays to Goddess Bhavani for Krishna as her husband. She then goes to the temple, where Krishna abducts her.

story.title: The Abduction of Rukmini
story.arc: Krishna's marriages and pastimes
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Rukmini sends a message to Krishna and before the wedding ceremony.
story.comic_potential: High; dramatic tension as Krishna arrives secretly, Rukmini's anxiety, the elaborate temple scene, and the sudden abduction.

teachings.teaching: Krishna reciprocates the devotee's love and acts swiftly to protect them.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'kriya' as divine action: when the soul (Rukmini) turns fully to God, God responds with immediate grace, overcoming all obstacles.

teachings.teaching: The devotee's single-minded focus on God leads to divine intervention.
teachings.lens_note: Rukmini's unwavering meditation on Krishna's feet (verse 40) exemplifies 'dhyana' (meditation) as a core Kriya-Yoga practice, which purifies the mind and attracts divine grace.

teachings.teaching: Surrender to God (prapatti) is the highest path.
teachings.lens_note: Rukmini's prayer to Bhavani, asking for Krishna as her husband, shows complete reliance on divine will, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga's bhakti approach.

chapter_summary: Rukmiṇī, adorned with jewels, walks gracefully to the temple, captivating the kings with her beauty. She boards Krishna's chariot, and Krishna abducts her in the presence of the assembled kings, who are enraged but helpless.

story.title: The Abduction of Rukmiṇī
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmiṇī
story.timeline_note: Immediately after Rukmiṇī's arrival at the temple.
story.comic_potential: High drama: Rukmiṇī's beauty stuns the kings, Krishna's swift abduction, and the kings' impotent rage.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's divine play (līlā) overcomes all obstacles.
teachings.lens_note: Krishna's abduction of Rukmiṇī demonstrates his supremacy and the futility of opposing him.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama defeat the allied kings, sparing Rukmi at Rukmini's plea. Balarama rebukes Krishna for disfiguring Rukmi and teaches Rukmini about the nature of the self.

story.title: The Defeat of the Kings and the Teaching to Rukmini
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmini and subsequent conflicts
story.timeline_note: Immediately after Krishna abducts Rukmini from her svayamvara.
story.comic_potential: Rukmi's boastful challenge and subsequent humiliation, with his beard and hair shaved off, has comedic elements.

teachings.teaching: The self is distinct from the body; the body is temporary and subject to change, while the self is eternal.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the practice of disidentifying from the body-mind complex to realize the true Self (Atman).

teachings.teaching: One should not be attached to dualities like pleasure and pain, as they are caused by ignorance.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes cultivating equanimity through Kriya-Yoga, transcending the pairs of opposites.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the sole controller; one should surrender to Him and not be disturbed by worldly events.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of Ishvara-pranidhana (surrender to God).

chapter_summary: Rukmiṇī's abduction by Kṛṣṇa is celebrated in Dvārakā with great festivity. The defeated kings, including Śiśupāla, are humiliated but Rukmī builds a new city, Bhojakaṭa, vowing not to return to Kuṇḍina without killing Kṛṣṇa. Kṛṣṇa marries Rukmiṇī according to Vedic rites, and the city rejoices.

story.title: Rukmiṇī's Abduction and Marriage
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa abducts Rukmiṇī, defeats rival kings, and marries her in Dvārakā, leading to celebration and Rukmī's exile.
story.timeline_note: Immediately after the abduction and defeat of the kings at Kuṇḍina.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic abduction, the defeated kings' humiliation, and the grand wedding celebration offer vivid scenes.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's actions are beyond material motives; He abducts Rukmiṇī to fulfill her desire and establish righteousness.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that divine actions are spontaneous (sahaja) and not bound by karma; the practitioner should act without attachment.

teachings.teaching: The defeated kings' anger and vows reflect the bondage of ego; Rukmī's building of Bhojakaṭa shows how pride leads to separation from the divine.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a warning against ahamkara (ego); the practitioner must surrender ego to attain union with Kṛṣṇa.

teachings.teaching: The celebration in Dvārakā symbolizes the joy of divine union; the city's decoration represents the inner purification when the soul unites with God.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the external festivity mirrors the internal bliss (ānanda) experienced when prana and consciousness unite.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of Pradyumna, the son of Krishna and Rukmini, who is an incarnation of Kamadeva. As a baby, he is stolen by the demon Shambara and thrown into the ocean, where a fish swallows him. The fish is caught and brought to Shambara's kitchen, where the child is discovered by Mayavati (Rati, Kamadeva's wife). She raises him and later reveals his identity. Pradyumna learns the Mahamaya vidya, defeats and kills Shambara, and returns to Dvaraka with Rati, reuniting with his family.

story.title: The Birth and Return of Pradyumna
story.arc: Pradyumna's abduction, growth in enemy's house, and victorious return
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's marriage to Rukmini and before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High: baby swallowed by fish, raised by enemy's cook, identity reveal, epic battle with magic, and dramatic reunion.

teachings.teaching: The divine plan unfolds even through apparent tragedies; the soul's journey is guided by higher purpose.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates how prarabdha karma is resolved through surrender to the divine will, and the 'fish' represents the material world that temporarily obscures the self.

teachings.teaching: True identity is not lost even when hidden; knowledge (vidya) dispels ignorance.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the Mahamaya vidya given by Mayavati symbolizes the Kriya-Yoga technique that destroys all illusions (maya) and reveals the true self.

teachings.teaching: The devotee's love (Rati) remains constant even when the beloved appears lost; reunion is inevitable.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of unwavering devotion (bhakti) as a means to reunite with the divine, overcoming separation caused by ignorance.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of the Syamantaka jewel. Satrajit, a devotee of the sun god, receives the jewel from Surya. His brother Prasena is killed by a lion while wearing it, and the lion is killed by Jambavan. Krishna is falsely accused of taking the jewel. To clear his name, Krishna tracks the path, finds the jewel in Jambavan's cave, fights Jambavan for 28 days, and is recognized as the Supreme Lord. Jambavan offers his daughter Jambavati and the jewel to Krishna. Krishna returns the jewel to Satrajit, who later gives his daughter Satyabhama and the jewel to Krishna, but Krishna declines the jewel.

story.title: The Syamantaka Jewel
story.arc: Krishna clears his name of false accusation, retrieves the Syamantaka jewel, and marries Jambavati and Satyabhama.
story.timeline_note: Occurs during Krishna's residence in Dwaraka, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: The mistaken identity of Satrajit as Surya, the fight with Jambavan, and the dramatic return from the cave.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects the reputation of His devotees and clears false accusations.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that the divine self (atman) actively removes obstacles and misperceptions that obscure its true nature, restoring harmony.

teachings.teaching: Material wealth (like the Syamantaka jewel) can cause conflict and false accusations; detachment is wise.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that attachment to objects (dravya) leads to bondage; the jewel symbolizes the mind's tendency to cling to external glitter, which must be surrendered.

teachings.teaching: True recognition of the divine comes through direct experience and surrender, not mere external appearance.
teachings.lens_note: Jambavan's fight and subsequent recognition of Krishna as the Supreme Lord mirrors the Kriya-Yoga process of inner struggle leading to self-realization.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama visit Hastinapura to console the Pandavas and Kunti after the fire incident. Meanwhile, Satrajit is murdered by Shatadhanva for the Syamantaka jewel. Shatadhanva flees, pursued by Krishna and Balarama. Krishna kills Shatadhanva but the jewel is not found. Balarama goes to Mithila, while Krishna returns to Dwaraka. Later, Krishna retrieves the jewel from Akrura, clearing his name.

story.title: The Syamantaka Jewel and the Death of Shatadhanva
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dwaraka
story.timeline_note: After the Pandavas' escape from the lac house, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High: chase scene, jewel theft, mistaken identity, divine intervention.

teachings.teaching: Even the Lord undergoes apparent difficulties to teach dharma and protect devotees.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this shows that the divine descends to restore balance, and the practitioner must face challenges with faith.

teachings.teaching: Greed leads to destruction, as seen in Shatadhanva's murder of Satrajit.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes detachment; greed is a major obstacle to inner peace.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's actions are beyond human comprehension; even sages are deluded by Maya.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga teaching that the ultimate reality is beyond intellect and can only be known through direct experience.

teachings.teaching: One should not harbor enmity towards the Lord or His devotees, as it leads to ruin.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, cultivating devotion (bhakti) is essential; opposition to the divine is self-destructive.

teachings.teaching: The Syamantaka jewel symbolizes divine grace; it brings prosperity to the worthy but danger to the unworthy.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga teaches that spiritual gifts must be handled with purity; otherwise they become sources of trouble.

chapter_summary: Krishna visits the Pandavas in Indraprastha, marries Kalindi (daughter of Surya), and later marries Mitravinda and Satya (Nagnajiti) after subduing seven bulls. The chapter also recounts the burning of Khandava forest and gifts from Agni and Maya.

story.title: Krishna's Marriages and the Khandava Forest Episode
story.arc: Krishna's visit to Indraprastha and his marriages to Kalindi, Mitravinda, and Satya, with flashback to the Khandava forest burning.
story.timeline_note: Events occur after Krishna's establishment in Dvaraka, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Krishna subduing seven bulls with playful ease, and the humorous confusion of Duryodhana in Maya's assembly hall.

teachings.teaching: Krishna, as the Supreme Lord, is the ultimate refuge for the helpless (anatha-samshraya).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of surrendering the ego to the divine will, allowing the inner self to guide actions.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's actions are playful (lila) and effortless, as seen when Krishna subdues the bulls by expanding into seven forms.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the Kriya-Yoga concept of acting without attachment, where the yogi performs actions as a divine play, free from personal motive.

teachings.teaching: True happiness comes from union with the divine, as expressed by Satya's desire for Krishna as her husband.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that lasting bliss arises from inner communion with the Self, not from external objects.

chapter_summary: Krishna marries several princesses: Satya (daughter of Nagnajit), Bhadra (daughter of Srutakirti), Lakshmana (daughter of the Madra king), and thousands of other women rescued after killing the demon Bhauma. Arjuna assists in protecting the wedding party.

story.title: Krishna's Marriages to Satya, Bhadra, and Lakshmana
story.arc: Krishna's marriages and rescue of captive women
story.timeline_note: After killing Bhauma and rescuing women, Krishna marries them.
story.comic_potential: Arjuna's lion-like routing of kings who attack the wedding procession.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's marriages demonstrate dharma in action, protecting and providing for devotees.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'kriya' as righteous action that harmonizes material and spiritual duties.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about how Lord Kṛṣṇa killed the demon Bhauma (Naraka) and rescued the captive princesses. Śuka narrates: Indra informed Kṛṣṇa of Bhauma's atrocities. Kṛṣṇa, with Satyabhāmā, rode Garuḍa to Prāgjyotiṣapura, breached its defenses, killed the five-headed demon Mura and his seven sons, then slew Naraka with his disc. Earth (Bhūmi) offered Naraka's earrings and umbrella, and pleaded for her son's pardon. Kṛṣṇa granted him liberation, entered the city, found 16,000 captive princesses, and sent them to Dvārakā. He then went to Indra's heaven, returned the earrings, uprooted the pārijāta tree for Satyabhāmā, and later married all the princesses simultaneously by expanding into multiple forms.

story.title: The Slaying of Naraka and the Rescue of the Princesses
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes as the supreme lord protecting the world and establishing dharma
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Kṛṣṇa's marriage to Satyabhāmā and before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic battle scenes with five-headed demon, flying on Garuḍa, simultaneous marriages, and divine interventions.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects his devotees and destroys demons who oppress the world.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the inner purification where the divine will (Kṛṣṇa) destroys the ego's attachments (demons) and liberates the soul (princesses).

teachings.teaching: Even a demon's son can be liberated if he surrenders to the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that surrender (prapatti) is the ultimate Kriya-Yoga practice, dissolving all karmic bonds.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the supreme controller and the ultimate goal of all beings.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view that all practices culminate in realizing the self as part of the divine.

teachings.teaching: Material opulence and heavenly pleasures are insignificant compared to devotion to the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that true wealth is inner spiritual realization, not external possessions.

chapter_summary: Krishna teases Rukmini about her choice of him as husband, pretending to be unworthy and suggesting she marry a king instead. Rukmini responds with a passionate defense of her devotion, explaining that she chose him as the Supreme Lord despite his apparent poverty. Krishna then reveals his teasing was a test, praises her devotion, and assures her of his eternal love.

story.title: Krishna's Teasing of Rukmini
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini, early in their domestic life.
story.comic_potential: High: Krishna's mock self-deprecation and Rukmini's indignant response create a playful, romantic comedy.

teachings.teaching: The Supreme Lord is self-sufficient and not dependent on material wealth; true devotion transcends worldly considerations.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This echoes the principle of 'niṣkiñcana' (having nothing) as the highest state, where the yogi relies solely on the Self.

teachings.teaching: A devotee should not be swayed by external appearances or social status; the Lord's grace is the ultimate goal.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The teaching emphasizes 'ātma-labdhi' (self-realization) over external acquisitions.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's playful words may test a devotee's resolve, but true love remains unshaken.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This illustrates the 'līlā' (divine play) aspect, where apparent obstacles are opportunities for deepening surrender.

teachings.teaching: Material desires bind one to suffering; the devotee seeks only the Lord's lotus feet.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This aligns with the path of 'vairāgya' (detachment) and 'bhakti' (devotion) as the means to liberation.

chapter_summary: Rukmini expresses her deep love and devotion to Krishna, recounting how she chose him over other suitors and how Krishna rescued her. She playfully chides Krishna for his absence and praises his divine qualities. The chapter concludes with Krishna enjoying conjugal pastimes with Rukmini and his other queens, demonstrating his role as the ideal householder while remaining the supreme Lord.

story.title: Rukmini's Loving Exchange with Krishna
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as a householder
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini, during their early married life.
story.comic_potential: Rukmini's playful complaints and Krishna's affectionate responses create a light-hearted, intimate scene.

teachings.teaching: The Lord reciprocates the love of His devotees and enjoys loving exchanges with them.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of bhakti-yoga: the devotee's loving service evokes the Lord's presence and reciprocation, leading to spiritual union.

teachings.teaching: Even as the supreme Lord, Krishna perfectly performs the duties of a householder, setting an example for human beings.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that one can attain perfection by performing one's prescribed duties with detachment and devotion, as exemplified by Krishna.

chapter_summary: This chapter lists the sons of Krishna by his chief queens, describes the marriage of Aniruddha to Rocana (daughter of Rukmi), and narrates the dice game at Bhojakaṭa where Balarāma defeats Rukmi and the Kaliṅga king, leading to Rukmi's death.

story.title: The Sons of Krishna and the Dice Game at Bhojakaṭa
story.arc: Krishna's family and the conflict with Rukmi
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmiṇī, Rukmi bears a grudge but later gives his daughter Rocanā to Aniruddha. The dice game occurs during the wedding festivities.
story.comic_potential: The dice game where Balarāma defeats Rukmi and knocks out the Kaliṅga king's teeth has high comic potential, especially with the divine voice declaring the winner.

teachings.teaching: The queens of Krishna, though sixteen thousand, could not conquer his mind with their charms, showing his supreme self-control.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the yogi's mastery over the senses, where the mind remains undisturbed by external stimuli.

teachings.teaching: Even Brahmā and others do not know the full glory of Krishna, indicating his transcendence.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on direct experience beyond intellectual knowledge.

teachings.teaching: The queens served Krishna with devotion, performing various services, exemplifying surrender.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, such service is a form of karma-yoga, purifying the heart.

teachings.teaching: Rukmi, despite his enmity, gave his daughter to Aniruddha out of affection for his sister, showing the power of family bonds.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates how even negative emotions can be overridden by love, a step in emotional purification.

teachings.teaching: Balarāma's anger and violent response to insult demonstrates the principle of righteous indignation.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, controlled anger can be used to uphold dharma, but must be free from personal ego.

chapter_summary: King Parikshit asks Shuka about the marriage of Aniruddha to Usha, daughter of the demon Bana. Shuka narrates Bana's boon from Shiva, his pride, and Shiva's prophecy that his flag would fall when defeated by a equal. Usha dreams of Aniruddha, and her friend Citralekha identifies him and brings him from Dvaraka. They marry secretly. Bana discovers them, attacks Aniruddha, and binds him with serpent ropes. Usha laments.

story.title: The Marriage of Aniruddha and Usha
story.arc: Bana's pride leads to conflict with Krishna's family; Aniruddha is captured but eventually rescued.
story.timeline_note: After Vamana's incarnation; during Krishna's time in Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: Usha's dream romance, Citralekha's magical flight, Aniruddha's secret tryst, Bana's rage, and the cliffhanger of Aniruddha bound in serpent ropes.

teachings.teaching: Pride leads to downfall; Bana's arrogance is checked by Shiva's prophecy.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, pride (ahamkara) is a obstacle to union with the Divine; it must be surrendered through devotion and self-awareness.

teachings.teaching: Divine love transcends social boundaries; Usha and Aniruddha's union is preordained.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that true love is a reflection of the soul's longing for the Supreme; it is not bound by worldly conventions.

teachings.teaching: The power of yoga (yogic power) enables Citralekha to transport Aniruddha across distances.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the siddhi (perfection) attainable through Kriya-Yoga practice, where the yogi can transcend physical limitations.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna and Balarama lead the Vrishnis to rescue Aniruddha from Bana's fortress. A fierce battle ensues between Krishna and Shiva, with various warriors clashing. Bana's mother Kotara intervenes, and the two Jvaras (fevers) fight. Bana's thousand arms are cut off, but Shiva pleads for his devotee's life. Krishna spares Bana, leaving him with four arms as a boon, and Aniruddha is reunited with Usha.

story.title: The Rescue of Aniruddha and the Battle with Bana
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Aniruddha's capture by Bana; four months pass before the Vrishnis learn of it via Narada.
story.comic_potential: High: cosmic battle with gods, demons, and fevers; Bana's thousand arms cut off; Kotara's naked intervention; Jvara fight; Shiva's praise of Krishna.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the supreme controller and the ultimate refuge; even Shiva and Brahma surrender to Him.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the need to surrender the ego (ahamkara) to the inner Self (Atman), which is Krishna.

teachings.teaching: The material world is a manifestation of the Lord's Maya; those attached to family and possessions are deluded.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that Kriya-Yoga cuts through maya by stilling the mind and realizing the Self as distinct from body and possessions.

teachings.teaching: One who neglects the Lord's feet in favor of sense pleasures is pitiable and cheats himself.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's emphasis on constant remembrance of the divine (Ishvara-pranidhana) as a core practice.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's mercy is available even to demons like Bana when they surrender.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga stresses that grace is accessible to all who sincerely practice, regardless of past karma.

chapter_summary: After being blessed by Lord Shiva, Lord Krishna returns to his capital Dvaraka with his wives. The city is beautifully decorated, and he enters amidst the sounds of conches and drums, greeted by citizens, friends, and brahmanas. The chapter concludes by stating that one who remembers this victory of Krishna over Shiva in the morning will never face defeat.

story.title: Krishna's victorious return to Dvaraka after battling Shiva
story.arc: Krishna returns to his capital after a conflict with Shiva, who ultimately blesses him. The city celebrates his victory.
story.timeline_note: After the battle with Shiva, Krishna returns to Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: A grand procession with decorated streets, conches, and drums, as the victorious hero returns home.

teachings.teaching: Remembering Krishna's victory over Shiva in the morning ensures one will never face defeat.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the power of remembrance (smriti) as a means to align with the divine will and overcome obstacles. The morning recitation sets the tone for the day, invoking the protective and victorious energy of Krishna.

chapter_summary: King Nṛga, a generous king of the Ikṣvāku dynasty, once gave a cow to a brāhmaṇa without realizing it belonged to another brāhmaṇa. Both brāhmaṇas claimed the cow, and despite Nṛga's offers of compensation, neither accepted. As a result, Nṛga was cursed to become a lizard in a dry well. After a long time, Lord Kṛṣṇa and the Yadu boys found him, and Kṛṣṇa lifted him out. Touched by Kṛṣṇa's hand, Nṛga regained his divine form and recounted his story. Kṛṣṇa then taught the Yadus about the severe consequences of misappropriating brāhmaṇa property.

story.title: The Deliverance of King Nṛga
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: Occurs during Kṛṣṇa's residence in Dvārakā, after the defeat of Bāṇāsura (Chapter 63).
story.comic_potential: High: The scene of Yadu boys finding a giant lizard in a well, Kṛṣṇa lifting it with one hand, and the lizard transforming into a radiant king is visually striking. The moral lesson about brahma-sva adds depth.

teachings.teaching: Misappropriating a brāhmaṇa's property, even unknowingly, leads to severe karmic consequences, including hellish suffering for many years.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this highlights the principle of karma and the importance of purity in action (kriya). Even unintentional wrongs must be atoned for, emphasizing vigilant awareness in all deeds.

teachings.teaching: One should never harm a brāhmaṇa, even if he is at fault; instead, one should bow to him always.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on humility and respect for spiritual authority as a means to purify the ego and advance on the path.

teachings.teaching: The property of a brāhmaṇa (brahma-sva) is like poison; it destroys the taker's family for generations.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a warning against coveting what belongs to the spiritually advanced, as it disrupts the subtle energy balance and leads to downfall.

chapter_summary: Balarama visits Nanda-gokula, is warmly received by the gopas and gopis, who express longing for Krishna. He consoles them with Krishna's messages, stays for two months, enjoys pastimes with the gopis, becomes intoxicated by the scent of Varuni, and in a drunken state, drags the Yamuna river with his plow when she disobeys his call. The river goddess submits, and Balarama releases her, then sports in the water. The chapter highlights Balarama's divine power and the gopis' devotion.

story.title: Balarama's Visit to Vraja and His Pastimes
story.arc: Balarama's journey to Nanda-gokula, his interactions with the gopas and gopis, his intoxication, and his display of power over the Yamuna.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's return to Mathura and Kamsa's death, during Balarama's visit to Vraja.
story.comic_potential: Balarama's drunken state, his dragging of the Yamuna river, and the gopis' emotional outbursts provide vivid, dramatic scenes.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' intense longing for Krishna illustrates the soul's yearning for the divine.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this longing represents the practitioner's deep desire for union with the inner Self, which is the essence of bhakti.

teachings.teaching: Balarama's intoxication and subsequent actions show that even divine beings can exhibit human-like behavior, teaching humility and the transcendence of divine play.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as a reminder that the divine is beyond moral judgments; the lila (play) of the Lord is a teaching tool to detach from worldly norms.

teachings.teaching: The Yamuna's submission to Balarama demonstrates the power of divine authority and the importance of surrender.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the surrender of the ego (the river) to the higher Self (Balarama), a key step in spiritual practice.

chapter_summary: Pauṇḍraka, a foolish king, claims to be Vāsudeva and sends a messenger to Kṛṣṇa demanding he surrender. Kṛṣṇa laughs, warns him, and then kills Pauṇḍraka and his ally the king of Kāśī in battle. The king of Kāśī's son Sudakṣiṇa performs a ritual to create a fiery demon to avenge his father, but Kṛṣṇa's Sudarśana cakra destroys the demon and burns Vārāṇasī. The chapter ends with the cakra returning to Kṛṣṇa.

story.title: The Killing of Pauṇḍraka and the Burning of Vārāṇasī
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes as the supreme lord, demonstrating his power over false claimants and protecting his devotees.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Rāma's return to Nanda-vraja, during Kṛṣṇa's residence in Dvārakā.
story.comic_potential: High; the absurdity of Pauṇḍraka's imitation, the dramatic battle, and the fiery demon provide vivid imagery.

teachings.teaching: False pride and imitation of the divine lead to destruction.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the danger of ego (ahamkara) claiming identity with the Self, which must be surrendered for true liberation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects his devotees and destroys the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the divine responds to sincere surrender (śaraṇāgati), as seen when Kṛṣṇa assures the frightened citizens.

teachings.teaching: Rituals performed with wrong intent (abhicāra) backfire on the performer.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the law of karma; the fire demon turns on Sudakṣiṇa, showing that actions motivated by hatred yield negative results.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about Balarāma's extraordinary deeds. Śuka narrates how the demon monkey Dvivida, friend of Naraka, terrorized the earth, especially Ānarta where Kṛṣṇa lived. Dvivida disrupted sages' hermitages, imprisoned people, and finally confronted Balarāma on Raivataka mountain. After a fierce battle, Balarāma killed Dvivida with his fist, praised by celestials.

story.title: The Slaying of Dvivida
story.arc: Balarāma's pastimes
story.timeline_note: After Naraka's death, Dvivida seeks revenge.
story.comic_potential: Dvivida's lewd gestures and Balarāma's drunken singing provide comic contrast.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects the righteous and destroys the wicked, even when the wicked are powerful.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of pratyāhāra: withdrawing from negative influences and letting the divine force (kriyā) eliminate obstacles.

teachings.teaching: One should not be arrogant or misuse power; Dvivida's pride led to his downfall.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes humility (vinaya) as essential for spiritual progress; arrogance (dambha) is a major obstacle.

chapter_summary: Samba, son of Krishna and Jambavati, abducts Lakshmana, daughter of Duryodhana, from her svayamvara. The Kauravas capture him. Balarama goes to Hastinapura to negotiate but is insulted. He becomes angry and threatens to destroy the city. The Kauravas surrender and offer gifts, and Balarama forgives them.

story.title: The Abduction of Lakshmana and Balarama's Intervention
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the events of the Mahabharata war? Actually, this is before the war, as Duryodhana is alive and ruling.
story.comic_potential: High: Balarama's anger and the threat to drag the city into the Ganga is visually dramatic.

teachings.teaching: Balarama's anger is for the sake of teaching the Kauravas a lesson, not out of hatred or envy.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that divine anger is a form of compassionate discipline, purifying the ego of the offenders.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the sole cause of creation, maintenance, and dissolution, and all beings are like toys in his play.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that the practitioner should see all events as the Lord's play (lila), cultivating detachment and surrender.

teachings.teaching: The Kauravas' pride in their royal status is condemned; true sovereignty belongs to the Lord alone.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's teaching of humility and recognizing the divine as the true source of all power.

chapter_summary: After receiving all the gifts, Lord Balarama, accompanied by his son and daughter-in-law, departed, honored by his friends. He entered his city and, meeting with his affectionate relatives, recounted all his deeds in the assembly of the Yadu chiefs. Even today, the city shows a raised southern bank along the Ganges, indicating Rama's prowess.

story.title: Balarama's Return to Dvaraka
story.arc: Balarama's journey and return after the abduction of Rukmini
story.timeline_note: After the events of the abduction of Rukmini and Balarama's intervention.
story.comic_potential: Balarama's triumphant return and the lasting mark on the landscape.

teachings.teaching: The importance of recounting one's deeds to inspire others.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the practice of svadhyaya (self-study) and sharing experiences to reinforce dharma.

chapter_summary: Narada visits Dvaraka to see how Krishna simultaneously lives with his 16,108 wives. He witnesses Krishna in each palace, engaged in different activities, demonstrating his yoga-maya. Narada praises Krishna and departs, having understood the divine play.

story.title: Narada's Visit to Dvaraka: The Yoga-Maya of Krishna
story.arc: Krishna's divine play and simultaneous presence in multiple households
story.timeline_note: After the defeat of Narakasura and the marriage of Krishna to many wives.
story.comic_potential: Narada's bewildered reactions as he sees Krishna in different roles, from playing dice to performing sacrifices, highlight the humorous aspect of divine multitasking.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's simultaneous presence in all households demonstrates his yoga-maya, the divine power to be everywhere at once.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'sarvatra sama-darśana' – seeing the same divine consciousness in all beings and activities, transcending duality.

teachings.teaching: Narada's humility and devotion: he bows to Krishna and accepts his role as a singer of the Lord's glories.
teachings.lens_note: This exemplifies the Kriya-Yoga attitude of surrender (prapatti) and service (sevā) as the highest path.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's statement that he is the speaker, doer, and approver of dharma, and he acts to teach the world.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the guru's role: the enlightened one acts in accordance with dharma to guide others, even while being beyond it.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes Krishna's daily routine in Dvaraka, his morning activities including meditation, worship, and charity, followed by his arrival at the Sudharma assembly hall. A messenger from imprisoned kings arrives seeking Krishna's help against Jarāsandha. Nārada then appears and informs Krishna about Yudhiṣṭhira's plan to perform the Rājasūya sacrifice, requesting his approval. Krishna consults Uddhava.

story.title: Krishna's Daily Routine and the Imprisoned Kings' Plea
story.arc: Preparation for the Rājasūya sacrifice and the liberation of kings from Jarāsandha
story.timeline_note: Early morning to midday in Dvaraka; Nārada's visit occurs after the assembly.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's elaborate morning routine contrasted with the sudden appearance of a distressed messenger and the dramatic entrance of Nārada.

teachings.teaching: Krishna meditates on the Supreme Brahman, the self-luminous, immutable reality beyond darkness.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the practitioner's daily meditation on the inner Self (Atman) as the source of all light and consciousness, transcending the three gunas.

teachings.teaching: One should perform daily duties (sandhyā, fire sacrifice, japa) with a calm mind.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga includes disciplined daily practice (niyama) as a foundation for higher meditation.

teachings.teaching: Charity to brahmins, cows, and the needy is an essential part of spiritual life.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views such acts as purifying the heart and reducing ego, preparing the practitioner for deeper yoga.

teachings.teaching: The six waves (ṣaḍ-ūrmi) of material existence (hunger, thirst, grief, delusion, old age, death) do not affect those who take shelter in Krishna's assembly.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the state of a realized yogi who, through Kriya-Yoga, transcends bodily identification and remains undisturbed by the dualities.

teachings.teaching: The kings lament that material happiness is like a dream, dependent on others, and they suffer due to māyā, abandoning the true happiness within.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma highlights that Kriya-Yoga aims to turn the mind inward to discover the innate bliss of the Self, which is independent of external circumstances.

teachings.teaching: Nārada states that hearing, chanting, and meditating on Krishna's glories purify even the lowest, and seeing and touching are even more powerful.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma sees this as the power of satsaṅga and nāma-saṅkīrtana, which are integral to Kriya-Yoga for purifying the mind and awakening devotion.

chapter_summary: Uddhava advises Krishna to kill Jarāsandha to enable the Rājasūya sacrifice, and Krishna agrees. He travels to Indraprastha, is warmly received by the Pāṇḍavas, and stays for several months.

story.title: Uddhava's Counsel and Krishna's Journey to Indraprastha
story.arc: Preparation for Rājasūya
story.timeline_note: After the events of Chapter 70, before the Rājasūya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: The grand procession of Krishna's army and the emotional reunion with the Pāṇḍavas.

teachings.teaching: The killing of Jarāsandha is necessary for the Rājasūya sacrifice and for the welfare of many.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the necessity of removing obstacles (karmic impurities) through decisive action (kriya) to achieve higher spiritual goals.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the ultimate cause of creation and destruction, and even deities like Brahmā and Śiva are His manifestations.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that the practitioner must recognize the supreme Self (Krishna) as the source of all powers and surrender to Him.

teachings.teaching: The devotees sing of Krishna's deeds, which include the killing of enemies and liberation of the soul.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the Kriya-Yoga principle of constant remembrance (smaraṇa) of the divine, which purifies the mind.

chapter_summary: Yudhiṣṭhira expresses his desire to perform the Rājasūya sacrifice. Kṛṣṇa advises him to first conquer all kings. Yudhiṣṭhira sends his brothers to subdue the directions. They bring tribute. Jarāsandha remains unconquered. Kṛṣṇa, Bhīma, and Arjuna, disguised as brāhmaṇas, go to Girivraja. They request a duel from Jarāsandha. After a long mace fight, Bhīma kills Jarāsandha with Kṛṣṇa's signal. Kṛṣṇa releases the imprisoned kings and installs Jarāsandha's son Sahadeva as king of Magadha.

story.title: The Slaying of Jarāsandha
story.arc: Preparation for the Rājasūya sacrifice
story.timeline_note: After the Pāṇḍavas' exile and before the great war.
story.comic_potential: High: Disguised heroes, a boastful villain, a dramatic duel with a secret signal, and a gruesome but just kill.

teachings.teaching: The Rājasūya sacrifice is the best of sacrifices and pleases all beings.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, performing such a sacrifice symbolizes the integration of all life forces (prāṇas) into the central channel (suṣumṇā), leading to spiritual sovereignty.

teachings.teaching: One should conquer the mind and senses before undertaking great actions.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that conquering external kings mirrors conquering internal enemies like desire and anger through disciplined practice (kriya).

teachings.teaching: The body is impermanent; use it to attain eternal fame through righteous deeds.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga principle of utilizing the temporary body for spiritual evolution, transforming mortality into immortality.

teachings.teaching: Even great kings like Bali gave up everything for brāhmaṇas, showing the value of charity and humility.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, serving the guru (represented by brāhmaṇas) is essential for spiritual progress; surrender leads to grace.

chapter_summary: Krishna releases 20,800 kings imprisoned by Jarasandha. They praise Krishna, renounce worldly desires, and seek devotion. Krishna instructs them on the dangers of pride and the path of bhakti. After honoring them, he returns to Indraprastha with Bhima and Arjuna, where Yudhishthira rejoices.

story.title: Liberation of the Imprisoned Kings
